Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjectival intensifier + evaluative adjective + noun (fixed nominal phrase)
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be extremely happy or deeply pleased
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common across Lower Columbia communities; tumtum metaphorically represents heart/mind
Phonetic Transcription (IPA): ňąhajas ňąk…¨u Ée ňąt į…ômt į…ôm
First Example Sentence: Nika hyas kloshe tumtum pus mika chako.
Second Example Sentence: Yaka hyas kloshe tumtum kopa ukuk wawa.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To be indifferent or emotionally detached
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Widely used in trade-era speech indicating lack of obligation or concern
First Example Sentence: Yaka halo tumtum kopa ukuk wawa.
Second Example Sentence: Nika halo tumtum pus klaska wawa hyas.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To feel emotionally hurt or upset
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Sick used metaphorically in Jargon contact contexts
First Example Sentence: Nika tumtum sick pus yaka halo wawa kloshe.
Second Example Sentence: Yaka tumtum sick kopa ukuk mamuk.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Mamuk as causative verb central to Chinook Jargon grammar
Phonetic Transcription (IPA): ňąmamuk ňąk…¨u Ée ňąt į…ômt į…ôm
First Example Sentence: Yaka mamuk kloshe tumtum kopa nika.
Second Example Sentence: Nsaika mamuk kloshe tumtum pus klaska tumtum sick.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in trade negotiations and humorous exchanges
First Example Sentence: Yaka hyas wawa kopa yaka mamuk.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To intrude or insert oneself unexpectedly
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Directional particles reflect spatial orientation in Lower Columbia dialects
First Example Sentence: Yaka chako k įupa kopa nsaika meeting.
Second Example Sentence: Klaska chako k įupa pus halo tiki.
Third Example Sentence: Nika nanich yaka chako k įupa alta.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Kumtuks from Nuu-chah-nulth influence meaning 'to know'
Second Example Sentence: Klaska halo kumtuks pus mamuk kloshe.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To hold high status or moral authority
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Saghalie (above) metaphorically linked to spiritual authority
First Example Sentence: Chief yaka mitlite saghalie kopa illahee.
Second Example Sentence: Yaka mitlite saghalie pus yaka mamuk kloshe.
Third Example Sentence: Klaska wawa pus yaka mitlite saghalie.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To favor or feel warmly toward someone
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects relational grammar in Jargon interpersonal speech
Phonetic Transcription (IPA): ňąt į…ômt į…ôm ňąk…¨u Ée ňąk įopa
Second Example Sentence: Yaka tumtum kloshe kopa ukuk illahee.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adverbial intensifier + transitive verb (fixed idiomatic use)
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Hyas intensifier common in Lower Columbia speech communities
First Example Sentence: Halo hyas mamuk kopa ukuk business.
Second Example Sentence: Yaka hyas mamuk pus yaka tumtum sick.
Third Example Sentence: Nsaika wawa pus halo hyas mamuk.
